response.scoreModifierReason: Self-identified lack of financial control at 18-24 with unknown debt and savings signals higher behavioral risk than demographics alone capture.
response.summary: You're early-career in FL, renting on a modest income, and the only thing you told us is that you're bad with money — which is a vibe, not a budget. The good news: you're young enough that fixing this now pays off more than almost any raise you'll ever get. The bad news: "bad with money" doesn't fix itself.
response.roast: You submitted "I'm just bad with money" like that's a financial plan. No numbers, no receipts, just a confession and a shrug — your bank account is somewhere crying and you don't even know how much it's crying.
insights: You gave zero numbers — which usually means you're avoiding looking, and avoidance is how small problems become big ones.
At 18-24, every dollar you redirect toward savings compounds harder than it ever will again — time is your only real edge.
Renting in FL on a modest income means your rent-to-income ratio is likely already tight, leaving little room for drift.
"Bad with money" is a behavior, not an identity — it's fixable, but only once you can see where the money actually goes.
Unknown debt and unknown savings brackets are red flags: you can't fix what you refuse to measure.
topProblems: No visibility into income, spending, or debt — you can't course-correct blind.
Self-described poor money habits with no current system or tracking in place.
Likely thin or nonexistent emergency fund given age, income bracket, and unknown savings.
positiveBehaviors: You admitted the problem out loud — most people in denial don't even get this far.
You're employed full-time at 18-24, which gives you a real foundation to build on.

topFix.action: Spend 20 minutes this week pulling your last two bank statements and writing down every outflow — you cannot make a plan until you know the actual numbers.
